                     EMPLOYMENT
                     AND EARNINGS
                                                                VOL. 17                NO. 3 SEPTEMBER 1 9 7 0


                     Joseph M. Finerty, Editor
                     John E. Bregger, Associate Editor

                                    Editors' Note
                                This issue of Employment and Earnings introduces an entirely revised series of
                                charts, presenting labor force activity over time. The charts reflect a number of
                                improvements which the Bureau hopes will stimulate increased interest in our
                                graphic presentation. The charts are more comprehensive in scope, in that they
                                cover more aspects of the manpower field than heretofore, and include time
                                series previously not published in this volume. In addition, the charts should
                                prove to be more useful for economic analysis, both on a current and long-term
                                basis. The editors welcome all comments, including suggestions for further im-
                                provement and expansion of the chart series.

                                                                    CAUTIONARY NOTE
                                        Job vacancy data are compiled from a sample of reporting establishments and may differ
                                    from the figures that would have been obtained if it were possible to take a complete
                                    census using the same questionnaries and procedures. Small differences in job vacancy
                                    rates between metropolitan areas, industries and occupations, therefore, may not be
                                    significant.
                                       Caution should be exercised in using these data to form conclusions in the absence of
                                    other supporting facts. As with any new statistical program, there is not yet enough ex-
                                    perience as to the relationships between job vacancy data and employment, unemploy-
                                    ment, labor turnover, and other economic data under varying economic conditions to
                                    warrant firm conclusions.
                                       The job vacancy data derived from the job vacancy survey differ from the unfilled
                                    job openings information compiled by the State Employment Security Agencies from
                                    their administrative records. The latter series reflect only the job openings listed with
                                    the Public Employment Service, and include all nonagricultural sectors as well as jobs not
                                    immediately available for filling.




Digitized for FRASER
http://fraser.stlouisfed.org/
Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis
                                                        Technical Description


                      The statistics for the first release of the job   with public or private employment agencies and
                   vacancy survey were compiled from monthly            school placement offices; notification to labor
                   questionnaries submitted by a sample of estab-       unions and professional organizations; "help
                   lishments to cooperating State employment se-        wanted" advertising (newspaper, posted notices,
                   curity agencies. These questionnaries, Form DL       etc.); recruitment programs; and interview and
                   1219—Monthly Report on Job Openings and Labor        selection of applicants.
                   Turnover, are of the "shuttle" type, with space
                   for each month of the calendar year. The collect-    Long-Term Job Vacancies
                   ing State agency returns the questionnaire to the
                   respondent each month so that the next month's          The reporting establishment is asked to in-
                   data can be entered.                                 dicate the number of current job vacancies which
                                                                        have continued unfilled for 30 days or more.
                   Concepts
                                                                        Job Vacancy Rate
                   Current Job Vacancies
                                                                           The job vacancy rate is computed by dividing
                       The reporting establishments submit job va-      the number of job vacancies by the sum of em-
                   cancy information as of the close of the last        ployment plus vacancies, and multiplying that
                   business day (or nearest possible day) of the        quotient by 100.
                   reference month. A current job vacancy is de-
                   fined as a job that is immediately available for     E mployment
                   filling, and for which the firm is actively trying
                   to find or recruit a worker from outside the            Employment data reported on Form DL 1219
                   firm.                                                refer to persons on establishment payrolls who
                                                                        received pay for any part of the pay period
                      Included are openings for all kinds of posi-      which included the 12th of the month. The em-
                   tions, classifications and employment, full-time,    ployment figures are used as weighting factors
                   part-time, permanent, temporary, and seasonal.       in estimating job vacancies.

                      Excluded are jobs to be filled by recall from     Industrial, Occupational and
                   layoff, transfer, promotion, demotion, or return     Area Classification
                   from paid or unpaid leave; job unoccupied be-
                   cause of labor-management disputes; job open-           The classification of establishments by indus-
                   ings for which "new" workers were already hired      try and by Standard Metropolitan Statistical Area
                   and scheduled to start work later; and openings      are the same as the labor turnover and payroll
                   with future starting dates, which are requested      employment statistics published regularly in
                    as a separate item.                                 Employment and Earnings. The classifications
                                                                        of vacancies by occupation are made in accord-
                      "Actively trying to find or recruit" means        ance with those established in the Dictionary of
                   that the establishment is engaged in efforts to      Occupational Titles, Third Edition, U.S. Depart-
                   fill the job vacancies by means of orders listed      ment of Labor, 1965.

                  SEASONALLY ADJUSTED EMPLOYMENT

                                              They all depend on the authoritative articles,
                                    reports, and statistical tables available exclusively in the
                                    Monthly Labor Review.


                                            Monthly Labor Review is the official source of data and
                                            analysis, on the Consumer Price Index and the Whole-
                                            sale Price Index.

                                            Monthly Labor Review is the authoritative journal of fact
                                            and analysis on employment and unemployment.

                                            Monthly Labor Review is the impartial recorder of
                                            changes in wages and fringe benefits, hours and earn-
                                            ings, productivity and unit labor costs.

                                            Monthly Labor Review publishes timely reports on col-
                                            lective bargaining in private industry and public em-
                                            ployment, plus monthly listings of 'major agreements
                                            expiring.

                                            Monthly Labor Review features analytical articles on
                                            significant court decisions, manpower planning, re-
                                            gional development and labor developments abroad.

                                            Monthly Labor Review offers thoughtful reviews and
                                            timely listings of current books in the fields of eco-
                                            nomics and the social sciences.




                                            MONTHLY LABOR REVIEW
                                            Bureau of Labor Statistics, U.S. Department of Labor


                                            For a 1-year subscription, send $9 to any BLS regional office or to:
                                            Superintendent of Documents, Government Printing Office, Washington,
                                            D.C. 20402. (Outside the USA, add $2.25.) Make checks payable to
                                            Superintendent of Documents.

                                                                   Technical Note

                                                       Additional information concerning the preparation
                                                       of the labor force, employment, hours and earnings,
                                                       and labor turnover series—concepts and scope,
                                                       survey methods, and limitations—is contained in
                                                       technical notes for each of these s e r i e s , available
                                                       f r o m the Bureau of Labor Statistics f r e e of charge.

                   Benchmark Adjustments
                                                                                      THE SAMPLE
                       Employment estimates a r e compared periodically
                   with comprehensive counts of employment which provide              Design

                   employment data, by industry, compiled quarterly by                size of establishment in t e r m s of employment. For each
                   State agencies f r o m reports of establishments covered           industry, the number of sample units is distributed
                   under State unemployment insurance laws. These tabu-               among the size class cells on the basis of average em-
                   lations, covering three-fourths of the total nonagricultural       ployment per establishment in each cell. In practice, this
                   employment in the United States, a r e prepared under the          is equivalent to distributing the predetermined total num-
                   direction of the Manpower Administration. Benchmark                ber of establishments required in the sample among the
                   data for the residual are obtained from the records of the         cells on the basis of the ratio of employment in each cell

                   Commission, and a number of other agencies in private              certainty stratum the sample members a r e selected at
                   industry or government.                                            random.

                                                             UNEMPLOYMENT INSURANCE DATA

                        Insured unemployment represents the number of per-        vacations. The rate of insured unemployment is the
                   sons reporting a week of unemployment under an unem-           number of insured unemployed expressed as a percent
                   ployment insurance program. It includes some persons           of average covered employment in a 12-month period
                   who are working part time who would be counted as em-          ending 6 to 8 months prior to the week of reference. Initial
                   ployed in the payroll and household surveys. Excluded          claims are notices filed by those losing jobs covered by
                   a r e persons who have exhausted their benefit rights          an unemployment insurance program that-they are starting
                   and workers who have not earned rights to unemployment         a period of unemployment. A claimant who continues to
                   insurance. In general, excluded from coverage are those        be unemployed a full week is then counted in the insured
                   persons who worked in firms whose size excluded them           unemployment figure.
                   from the unemployment insurance laws, as well as many              Because of differences in State laws and procedures
                   persons engaged in agriculture, domestic service, unpaid       under which unemployment insurance programs are op-
                   family work, selected nonprofit organizations, State and       erated, State unemployment rates generally indicate, but
                   local government and self-employment. Also excluded            do not precisely measure, differences among the indi-
                   from the insured unemployment count, but included as           vidual States. Persons wishing to receive a detailed
                   employed in the household survey, are those persons who        description of the nature, sources, inclusions and exclu-
                   earned no wages during the payroll period because they         sions, and limitations of unemployment insurance data
                   were temporarily absent from their jobs due to taking          should address their inquiries to Manpower Administra-
                   time off, illness and industrial disputes as well as unpaid     tion, Wasnington, D.C. 20210.

                                                                                 ATTENTION
                        As discussed in the Technical Note, the Bureau periodically a d j u s t s the industry employment series to a recent
                   benchmark to improve their accuracy. These adjustments may also affect the hours and earnings series because employ-
                   ment levels are used as weights. Industry data for all national series shown in this report have been adjusted to March
                   1969 benchmarks. Data from A p r i l 1969 forward are subject to revision at the time of the next benchmark.

                         Beginning with the June 1970 and subsequent i s s u e s of Employment and Earnings, the national data in sections B,
                   C, and D supersede those published in previous i s s u e s , as well as those appearing in the Handbook of Labor Statistics,
                   1970. Comparable data will be published in Employment and Earnings, United States, 1909-70, BLS Bulletin 1312-7.
